content.text: AWS GovCloud (US) is a set of isolated AWS regions designed to host sensitive government workloads. Unlike standard AWS regions that share infrastructure and routing with the public internet, GovCloud regions operate in a physically separate partition — a distinct network with separate endpoints, separate IAM, and access restricted to US persons and US-incorporated entities.

attrs.alt: AWS GovCloud dual-account architecture: commercial account linked for billing to isolated GovCloud account

content.text: US federal law and DoD policy impose strict constraints on where certain categories of data can be processed and by whom:
defense-related technical data, software, and services controlled by the US Department of State. Foreign persons cannot access ITAR-controlled data, even if they work at an otherwise-cleared company.
a government-defined category for sensitive but unclassified data — medical records, law enforcement data, export-controlled research. CUI requires NIST 800-171 controls.
the highest level of the Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program. Required for cloud systems processing law enforcement, emergency services, and healthcare data.
Department of Defense Impact Levels for classified and mission-critical data. IL5 is required for National Security Systems (NSS).

A standard AWS region cannot host ITAR-controlled data because AWS employees outside the US could potentially access the underlying hardware or support tickets. GovCloud restricts access to US persons only — including AWS staff.
FedRAMP HIGH, ITAR, CJIS, DoD IL2/IL4
FedRAMP HIGH, DoD IL5, IRS 1075, ITAR, CJIS
AWS partition, separate from the commercial
partition. The partition boundary is significant: IAM principals, resource ARNs, and API endpoints are all partition-scoped.
AWS divides its global infrastructure into partitions. Each partition has its own:
IAM identity namespace — a commercial IAM role ARN cannot be used in GovCloud.
API endpoints — us-gov-east-1 S3 endpoint is s3.us-gov-east-1.amazonaws.com, not s3.amazonaws.com.
Resource ARNs — GovCloud ARNs contain 'aws-us-gov': arn:aws-us-gov:s3:::my-bucket.
Certificate authorities — SSL certificates come from the partition's own CA chain.
Service availability — not all commercial services are available in GovCloud (see below).

# Commercial partition ARN format
arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket
arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/MyRole

# GovCloud partition ARN format
arn:aws-us-gov:s3:::my-bucket
arn:aws-us-gov:iam::123456789012:role/MyRole

You cannot create a standalone GovCloud account. AWS requires that every GovCloud account be linked to a commercial AWS account for billing purposes. The commercial account is the billing root; the GovCloud account is isolated for workloads.
Create a standard AWS commercial account.
Accept GovCloud terms of service and undergo AWS screening (US person / entity verification).
AWS creates a GovCloud account automatically linked to your commercial account.
Billing and cost management happen in the commercial console; workloads run in the GovCloud console.
The two accounts are separate AWS accounts with different account IDs. Resources, IAM, and data do not cross between them. The link is billing-only.
The GovCloud console and APIs use different URLs from the commercial partition:

# GovCloud console
https://console.amazonaws-us-gov.com/

# AWS CLI — configure a separate profile for GovCloud
[profile govcloud]
aws_access_key_id     = AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
aws_secret_access_key = wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY
region                = us-gov-east-1

# Use the profile
aws --profile govcloud ec2 describe-instances

# S3 endpoint (GovCloud)
aws --profile govcloud s3 ls s3://my-gov-bucket/
GovCloud IAM credentials are separate from commercial credentials. You cannot use a commercial access key to call GovCloud APIs, and vice versa.
GovCloud supports the most commonly needed services for government workloads, but the catalog is smaller than commercial regions. As of 2024:
EC2, ECS, EKS, Lambda, Batch, Fargate
S3, EBS, EFS, Glacier, Storage Gateway
RDS (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, Oracle), DynamoDB, ElastiCache, Redshift
VPC, Direct Connect, Route 53, CloudFront (limited), Global Accelerator
IAM, KMS, CloudTrail, GuardDuty, Security Hub, WAF, Shield
CodePipeline, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, CloudFormation, Systems Manager
Some newer services, AWS Marketplace (limited), some regions' services

Always verify service availability in the GovCloud Service Endpoints documentation before designing an architecture. New commercial services typically arrive in GovCloud 6–18 months after commercial launch.

GovCloud uses AWS KMS with keys that are stored and operated within the GovCloud partition. FIPS 140-2 Level 2 validated HSMs are used for all KMS operations in GovCloud — this is a compliance requirement for many federal programs.
(aws/s3, aws/ebs) are automatically created and rotated within GovCloud.
can be created with key material generated within GovCloud HSMs. You can enable automatic annual rotation.
is supported, but imported key material from a commercial KMS key cannot be used in GovCloud — keys are partition-isolated.
) for workloads that require FIPS-validated cryptographic modules.

# Create a CMK in GovCloud KMS
aws --profile govcloud kms create-key \
  --description 'ITAR-compliant encryption key' \
  --key-usage ENCRYPT_DECRYPT \
  --key-spec SYMMETRIC_DEFAULT

# Enable rotation
aws --profile govcloud kms enable-key-rotation \
  --key-id arn:aws-us-gov:kms:us-gov-east-1:123456789012:key/abc123

# Use FIPS endpoint for S3
aws --profile govcloud --endpoint-url \
  https://s3-fips.us-gov-east-1.amazonaws.com \
  s3 cp classified.pdf s3://my-itar-bucket/
All API calls in GovCloud are logged by CloudTrail. For FedRAMP and DoD compliance, you must enable CloudTrail in all GovCloud regions and retain logs for at least one year (three years for IL5):

# Enable CloudTrail in GovCloud region
aws --profile govcloud cloudtrail create-trail \
  --name govcloud-audit-trail \
  --s3-bucket-name my-govcloud-audit-logs \
  --is-multi-region-trail \
  --enable-log-file-validation

aws --profile govcloud cloudtrail start-logging \
  --name govcloud-audit-trail
The AWS Terraform provider supports GovCloud by configuring the provider with the GovCloud region and using gov-partition ARNs:
GovCloud vs Standard Region: Key Differences
US persons and US-incorporated entities only
code that embeds commercial ARNs (e.g., in IAM policy templates or CDK constructs) will fail in GovCloud. Use the partition-aware
SDKs default to commercial partition endpoints. Always configure the correct region (
third-party AMIs and software from AWS Marketplace may not be available in GovCloud. Evaluate alternatives or bring your own license.
you cannot assume a role across partitions. Commercial IAM roles cannot assume GovCloud roles.
commercial stack sets cannot deploy to GovCloud accounts. Maintain separate stack set configurations for GovCloud.
if your architecture depends on a recently launched commercial service, check GovCloud availability before committing to the design.
To write code that works in both commercial and GovCloud without duplication, use the AWS SDK's partition-aware helpers:

# Python boto3: partition-aware endpoint resolution
import boto3

session = boto3.Session(profile_name='govcloud', region_name='us-gov-east-1')
s3 = session.client('s3')

# boto3 automatically uses the correct endpoint for the configured region
# s3.us-gov-east-1.amazonaws.com — no manual endpoint needed
response = s3.list_buckets()
print([b['Name'] for b in response['Buckets']])

FedRAMP (Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program) standardizes security assessment and authorization for cloud services used by the federal government. Understanding which FedRAMP authorization level your workload requires determines whether GovCloud is necessary or if a standard region suffices.
Public data — impact of breach is limited
Sensitive but non-classified — PII, financial records
FBI case data, 911 systems, VA patient records

The key distinction: FedRAMP Moderate means the confidentiality, integrity, or availability impact of a breach is moderate. FedRAMP HIGH means the impact would be severe — potentially catastrophic to government operations, public safety, or national security.
IAM in GovCloud functions identically to commercial IAM but is partition-isolated. Several operational differences matter in practice:
IAM users, roles, and access keys created in GovCloud accounts are not visible or usable from commercial accounts. Maintain separate credentials for each partition.
a commercial IAM role cannot appear in a GovCloud resource policy.
is not a valid principal in a GovCloud bucket policy.
supports GovCloud. Deploy the Identity Center instance in GovCloud to manage SSO for your GovCloud accounts with SAML federation to an on-premises IdP.
use permission boundaries on all GovCloud roles to prevent privilege escalation — a common FedRAMP control requirement (AC-6 least privilege).
if using AWS Organizations with GovCloud accounts, SCPs enforce guardrails across all GovCloud accounts — for example, deny any action outside us-gov-east-1.
GovCloud VPCs and networking work like commercial but with stricter isolation requirements:
government agencies frequently use AWS Direct Connect to GovCloud, bypassing the public internet entirely. This is required for some DoD IL5 workloads.
Site-to-Site VPN is available. For FIPS compliance, configure the VPN to use FIPS 140-2 validated algorithms (AES-256-GCM, SHA-384).
No public internet gateway for classified:
IL5 architectures often prohibit internet gateway routes. Use VPC endpoints for all AWS service access and Direct Connect for external connectivity.
all major GovCloud services have interface VPC endpoints. S3 and DynamoDB use gateway endpoints. This keeps traffic off the internet.
limited in GovCloud. Some edge caching use cases require alternative CDN solutions or accepting higher latency from the region directly.

# Create S3 VPC gateway endpoint in GovCloud VPC
aws --profile govcloud ec2 create-vpc-endpoint \
  --vpc-id vpc-0abc123 \
  --service-name com.amazonaws.us-gov-east-1.s3 \
  --route-table-ids rtb-0xyz456

# Create KMS interface endpoint (for FIPS)
aws --profile govcloud ec2 create-vpc-endpoint \
  --vpc-id vpc-0abc123 \
  --vpc-endpoint-type Interface \
  --service-name com.amazonaws.us-gov-east-1.kms-fips \
  --subnet-ids subnet-0private \
  --security-group-ids sg-0endpoint

AWS Security Hub in GovCloud aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and Config, and maps them to compliance frameworks. For federal agencies, the NIST SP 800-53 standard in Security Hub is particularly useful:

# Enable Security Hub with NIST 800-53 standard in GovCloud
aws --profile govcloud securityhub enable-security-hub \
  --enable-default-standards

aws --profile govcloud securityhub batch-enable-standards \
  --standards-subscription-requests \
  StandardsArn=arn:aws-us-gov:securityhub:::ruleset/nist-800-53/v/5.0.0

# Get compliance summary
aws --profile govcloud securityhub get-findings \
  --filters 'ComplianceStatus=[{Value=FAILED,Comparison=EQUALS}]' \
  --query 'Findings[*].[Title,Severity.Label,Compliance.Status]' \
  --output table
Migrating existing workloads from a commercial region to GovCloud requires more than a snapshot restore — partition-level differences affect IAM, ARNs, and service endpoints throughout the stack:
scan all IAM policies, CloudFormation templates, and application configs for hardcoded commercial ARNs. Replace with partition-aware references.
GovCloud IAM is empty. Re-create all users, roles, and policies. Consider exporting policies from commercial and importing, but verify every principal ARN.
KMS keys are partition-specific. Decrypt data with the commercial key, transfer it to GovCloud, and re-encrypt with a GovCloud KMS key. For S3, use S3 Replication with re-encryption.
internal service discovery that references commercial endpoints must be updated. Route 53 private hosted zones in GovCloud are separate from commercial.
after migration, run Security Hub findings against NIST 800-53, enable Config rules for CIS benchmarks, and request a FedRAMP authorization package update.
Hardcoded commercial ARNs in application code
Use environment variables or Parameter Store for all ARNs; update at deploy time
Commercial AMIs not available in GovCloud
Rebuild AMIs in GovCloud using EC2 Image Builder or import via VM Import/Export
Third-party software without GovCloud Marketplace listing
BYOL (bring your own license) or contact vendor for GovCloud availability
Re-create permission sets; update SAML metadata in your IdP
AWS RAM is supported in GovCloud but cannot share across partitions
GovCloud services are typically priced higher than equivalent commercial services, reflecting the compliance overhead, physical isolation, and restricted staffing:
EC2 on-demand instances in GovCloud cost approximately 10–15% more than equivalent commercial instances.
S3 storage and request pricing is slightly higher in GovCloud than commercial us-east-1.
Data transfer pricing follows similar patterns to commercial regions.
There is no cost for linking a GovCloud account to a commercial account.
Reserved Instances and Savings Plans are available in GovCloud and provide similar 30–60% discounts over on-demand.
Billing consolidation happens in the commercial account — GovCloud charges appear in the commercial billing dashboard.
AWS GovCloud (US) is a physically isolated partition restricted to US persons, designed to host ITAR, CUI, FedRAMP HIGH, and DoD IL4/IL5 workloads.
Two GovCloud regions: us-gov-west-1 (Oregon) and us-gov-east-1 (Virginia, DoD IL5). Both are in the aws-us-gov partition.
Every GovCloud account must be linked to a commercial account for billing — the two accounts are otherwise completely isolated.
ARNs, IAM, API endpoints, and KMS keys are partition-scoped. Commercial and GovCloud resources cannot directly reference each other.
Use partition-aware code (data.aws_partition, boto3 region config) to write portable infrastructure that works in both commercial and GovCloud environments.
